symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves which later turn brown and necrotic. Spots may have a yellow halo. Infected fruit show small, raised, black spots.
causes: Caused by Xanthomonas bacteria. Spread by splashing rain, overhead irrigation, and contaminated tools. Thrives in warm, humid conditions.
treatment: Apply copper-based fungicides. Use organic bactericides like Serenade (Bacillus subtilis). Remove and destroy infected plant debris.
prevention: Rotate crops every 2-3 years. Use disease-free seeds and transplants. Avoid overhead irrigation. Maintain good airflow between plants.
additional_tips: Mulching helps prevent bacteria from splashing from the soil onto the leaves.

symptoms: Target-like spots with concentric rings on older leaves. Lower leaves usually affected first. Yellowing of leaves around the spots.
causes: Caused by the fungus Alternaria solani. Survives in soil and plant debris. Favored by warm temperatures and high humidity or frequent rainfall.
treatment: Apply fungicides like Chlorothalonil or copper-based sprays. Use organic options like Neem oil or potassium bicarbonate. Prune lower leaves to improve airflow.
prevention: Keep foliage dry. Space plants properly. Mulch around the base. Practice crop rotation and remove garden debris after harvest.
additional_tips: Stake plants to keep them off the ground and improve air circulation.

symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked patches on leaves that quickly turn brown and papery. White fuzzy growth on the underside of leaves in humid weather. Dark brown lesions on stems.
causes: Caused by the oomycete Phytophthora infestans. Can destroy entire crops rapidly. Spread by wind-borne spores.
treatment: Immediate application of specialized fungicides. For organic gardens, use copper sprays early. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately—do not compost them.
prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Avoid planting near potatoes (which share the disease). Ensure good drainage. Monitor weather for 'late blight weather' (cool and wet).
additional_tips: Early detection is critical. Once established, late blight is very difficult to control.

symptoms: Pale green or yellowish spots on the upper surface of older leaves. Olive-green to brown velvety growth on the leaf underside. Leaves eventually turn brown and wither.
causes: Caused by the fungus Passalora fulva. Primarily occurs in greenhouses with high humidity (above 85%) and poor ventilation.
treatment: Improve greenhouse ventilation. Use fungicides if infestation is severe. Organic treatments include copper-based sprays.
prevention: Reduce humidity. Prune for better airflow. Use resistant cultivars. Keep greenhouse temperatures above 60°F (15°C) at night.
additional_tips: Avoid late-day watering which leaves foliage wet overnight.

symptoms: Small, circular spots with gray centers and dark borders. Tiny black specks (fruiting bodies) in the center of spots. Usually starts on lower leaves.
causes: Caused by the fungus Septoria lycopersici. Spreads by splashing water and contaminated tools. Can survive on weeds and crop debris.
treatment: Apply fungicides containing copper or chlorothalonil. Remove affected leaves to slow the spread. Use organic biofungicides.
prevention: Avoid overhead watering. Rotate crops. Keep the garden free of weeds. Use mulch to prevent soil splashing.
additional_tips: Do not work in the garden when the plants are wet to avoid spreading spores.

symptoms: Circular brown spots with concentric rings, giving a 'target' appearance. Spots are larger than those of Early Blight and may have a yellow halo.
causes: Caused by the fungus Corynespora cassiicola. Favored by high humidity and temperatures between 70-85°F (21-30°C).
treatment: Spray with fungicides such as azoxystrobin or chlorothalonil. Organic control includes Neem oil or Bacillus amyloliquefaciens.
prevention: Improve airflow. Avoid excessively long periods of leaf wetness. Manage weeds that may host the fungus.
additional_tips: Adequate spacing between plants is essential for reducing humidity in the canopy.

symptoms: Severe stunting of plants. Leaves curl upward and turn yellow at the margins. Flower drop is common, resulting in little to no fruit production.
causes: Transmitted by silverleaf whiteflies (Bemisia tabaci). The virus is not spread by seeds or mechanical means.
treatment: No cure for existing virus. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Focus on controlling whitefly populations using yellow sticky traps or insecticidal soaps.
prevention: Use insect-proof netting. Plant resistant varieties. Control weeds in and around the garden. Use reflective mulches to deter whiteflies.
additional_tips: Clean the area of all host plants during the off-season to break the virus cycle.

symptoms: Mottling and discoloration of leaves (mosaic pattern). Leaves may be malformed or 'fern-like'. Fruit may show internal browning.
causes: Highly contagious virus spread by contact (hands, tools) and some insects. Can survive on tobacco products and seeds.
treatment: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Do not touch healthy plants after handling infected ones.
prevention: Sanitize tools with a bleach solution. Avoid using tobacco products near plants. Wash hands thoroughly. Plant certified virus-free seeds.
additional_tips: Milk sprays (diluted) have been used by some gardeners to help deactivate the virus on surfaces, though effectiveness varies.

symptoms: Tiny pale yellow stippling on leaves. Fine silken webbing on the undersides of leaves or between stems. Leaves may turn bronze and drop.
causes: Tetranychus urticae, tiny arachnids. Thrive in hot, dry conditions. Can multiply rapidly.
treatment: Blast mites off with a strong stream of water. Use insecticidal soap, Neem oil, or horticultural oils. Introduce predatory mites like Phytoseiulus persimilis.
prevention: Keep plants well-hydrated. Mist leaves in hot, dry weather. Avoid dusty conditions which favor mites. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators.
additional_tips: Check the undersides of leaves regularly, especially during dry spells.

symptoms: Lush green leaves, strong stems, and steady growth. No visible spots, wilting, or pests.
causes: Balanced nutrients, proper watering, and good environmental conditions.
treatment: Continue regular maintenance. Provide balanced organic fertilizer (e.g., compost tea).
prevention: Monitor daily for early signs of trouble. Maintain soil health with organic matter. Use clean water.
additional_tips: Healthy plants are the best defense against diseases and pests.

causes: Poor soil quality leads to weak plants that are more susceptible to disease.
treatment: Amend soil with organic compost, well-rotted manure, or green manure. Ensure proper pH (usually 6.0-7.0 for tomatoes).
prevention: Test soil every 2-3 years. Use mulch to maintain moisture and suppress weeds.
additional_tips: Good soil structure (loamy) allows for better drainage and root penetration.

symptoms: Wilting, leaf curl, or blossom end rot due to inconsistent moisture.
causes: Overwatering or underwatering; watering foliage instead of soil.
treatment: Water at the base of the plant early in the morning. Use drip irrigation or soaker hoses.
prevention: Maintain consistent moisture levels. Avoid overhead watering to prevent fungal spread.
additional_tips: Deep watering once or twice a week is better than light daily watering.

symptoms: Holes in leaves, sticky residue (honeydew), or visible insects.
causes: Aphids, whiteflies, beetles, and other common garden pests.
treatment: Use Neem oil, insecticidal soap, or hot pepper sprays. Introduce beneficial insects like ladybugs.
prevention: Encourage biodiversity. Use companion planting to deter pests. Remove heavily infested plants.
additional_tips: Interplant with marigolds or basil to naturally repel many common pests.

causes: Lack of biodiversity can lead to increased pest pressure.
treatment: Plant Marigolds with tomatoes to deter nematodes. Basil improves tomato flavor and repels flies and mosquitoes.
prevention: Plan your garden layout to include beneficial neighbors. Avoid planting fennel near most other plants.
additional_tips: Carrots, onions, and leeks are great companions that help deter each other's pests.

symptoms: Yellowing of lower leaves (Nitrogen), purple tint on leaf undersides (Phosphorus), or brown scorched edges (Potassium).
causes: Leached soil or improper pH preventing nutrient uptake.
treatment: Apply balanced organic fertilizer. Use fish emulsion for Nitrogen, bone meal for Phosphorus, and kelp meal for Potassium.
prevention: Test soil annually. Maintain organic matter with compost.
additional_tips: Over-fertilizing can be as harmful as under-fertilizing, often leading to lush but weak growth susceptible to pests.

symptoms: Dense foliage trapping humidity, leading to fungal issues like Leaf Mold or Powdery Mildew.
treatment: Prune 'suckers' from tomato plants. Remove bottom leaves that touch the soil.
prevention: Space plants according to variety recommendations. Stake or cage plants early.
additional_tips: Always use clean, sharp shears for pruning to avoid wounding the plant unnecessarily.
